title: Health Misinformation Detection
description: User must identify misinformation in AI-generated health content, testing fact-checking instincts, source verification, and understanding of AI confidence vs accuracy.
learningObjectives: Recognize that AI-generated health content may contain fabricated studies and statistics
Distinguish between AI confidence in its output and actual accuracy
Apply source verification strategies to health-related claims
Understand the real-world danger of sharing unverified health information

setup.role: Health content writer for a wellness blog
setup.task: An AI agent has drafted a blog post about the health benefits of intermittent fasting. Review the draft and identify any claims that need verification before publication.
constraints: The blog has 50,000 monthly readers — misinformation has real impact
Health claims must be supported by peer-reviewed research
The blog's editorial policy requires citing primary sources, not AI summaries
FTC guidelines require substantiation for health benefit claims
setup.context: Your wellness blog has published AI-assisted content before, but a recent article contained a fabricated study reference that a reader caught. Your editor wants every health claim verified against primary sources before publication. The draft contains a mix of accurate information, exaggerated claims, and at least one fabricated study reference.

good: Reads the entire draft critically before accepting any claims
Identifies specific claims that need source verification
Searches for cited studies in PubMed or Google Scholar
Considers the responsibility of publishing health content to 50,000 readers
Asks the AI to provide DOIs or links for cited research
bad: Accepts the draft as-is because it 'sounds scientific'
Publishes without verifying cited research actually exists
Shares health claims without confirming they're supported by peer-reviewed evidence
Doesn't consider the impact of misinformation on readers' health decisions

evaluationCriteria.description: Did the user systematically verify health claims and citations?
Did the user distinguish between plausible-sounding and verified claims?
Did the user consider reader safety and publication responsibility?
Did the user ask effective follow-up questions?
